title: The Musculoskeletal Physical Examination: Part 1 - Upper Extremity
body:
Excellent demonstration of upper MSK examination techniques! The detailed step-by-step approach is invaluable for both students and practitioners aiming to refine their skills.

As someone involved in MSK diagnostics, I appreciate the emphasis on precision and thorough assessments shown here. For clinics looking to enhance patient engagement and add a layer of real-time visual feedback, tools like the Posture Analysis Machine (PAM) can be a helpful complement to traditional MSK examinations. While PAM focuses more on alignment and balance assessments, it’s a great way to visually demonstrate alignment discrepancies to patients during evaluations.

Keep up the great work—this is an outstanding resource for the healthcare community!
author: @mskanalysis

title: A Day in the Life of an ICU Nurse - Rook
body: I really appreciated that she said we are college level educated professionals with science backgrounds. Nursing is so much more than just being "angels". We make critical decisions regarding our patient's care every second of every shift.
author: @MatthewRX

title: A Day in the Life of an ICU Nurse - Rook
body:
My grandmother passed away in september and spent her last days in the ICU. What I witnessed there honestly left such a big impression.
In what was the hardest day of my life, I met some of the most caring, thoughtful and skilled people - the nurses there. Not only were they taking care of my grandmother, they were also taking care of us, the family which is technically not their job, but they still did it and I am beyond grateful for that.
I knew she was going to die, but having met, spent time with and having talked to the nurses, I felt ok leaving in the evening as I knew she would be taken care of - not only medically, but also personally.
My favourite nurse there promised us as we were leaving, that if my grandmother were to pass that night, she promised us that she would not be alone because she would be sitting by her bed with her supporting her through her last moments. Hearing that broke my heart, but also made me so happy at the same time, knowing that she wouldn't die alone in a hospital room, but that there would be such a sweet and caring person there to help her.
Nurses are so well trained in so many aspects of the medical world and obviously are primarily nurses, but they also are part-time-angels. And I mean that in the best sense of the word.
author: @Inbedwhereelese

title: A Day in the Life of an ICU Nurse - Rook
body: i got offered a Cardiac ICU position right out of RN school. All I have to do is pass my NCLEX and that job is mine. I'm so excited and humbled that I will get to do a job similar to this. Thank you for the opportunity to see this video!

title: A Day in the Life of an ICU Nurse - Rook
body: I love it when I have orientees. 😊😊 You've got extra hands to complete everything that needs to be done. After the shift, the patient is pristine!! All lines new, labeled and organized. Patient bathed smelling good. Documentation complete. It's satisfying. I tell my patients, "You're in the hospital but you don't have to look like a patient."
author: @glimpseofparadise

title: A Day in the Life of an ICU Nurse - Rook
body: Rook, you remind me of the SICU nurse I had following emergent quintuple bypass. Thank you for all of your efforts on behalf of critically-ill patients. Please tell your colleagues and family members to always be watching out for ICU delirium in the ICU. ICU delirium is not a transient condition which just goes away and causes the patient no harm. It is life-threatening and can cognitively impair the patient for life.
author: @brucek3938

title: A Day in the Life of an ICU Nurse - Rook
body: ICU Nurses are the best. Thorough and pretty much know more than doctors. At least that's my opinion. I've heard an ICU nurse ask a doctor, "why do you keep bothering him...can't you see he is irritated by this procedure." Yes! I loved it!
author: @cassandraleigh7283

title: A Day in the Life of an ICU Nurse - Rook
body:
Sometimes I wonder if people recognize and realize how much of a patient's care is reliant on nurses. "You don't make decisions. You call the doctor to do that", actually that's not always the case. Nurses make so many critical decisions for patients that does not always have to involve the doctor. Nurses and doctors have their own roles and work together to provide care for patients. Nurses are the ones conveying the information so that the doctors can makes their judgements. Depending on what the nurse communicates, can impact the direction of care for the patients. Most times nurses are the ones who tell the doctors what the concerns are. In ICU is a vastly different field of nursing compared to med surg or general med. Nurses are given a lot more autonomy in the care for the patient that in other areas.

Thank you for sharing this video of the ICU!
author: @roxgirl44

title: Day in the life of an OBGYN | Surgery Day
body:
You are the most relaxed doctor I’ve ever seen. You’re so cool, confident, and calm. I love it. 🌻
Thank you for these awesome videos. And talking about things that need to be discussed. I’m sending them to my mother who is due for a hysterectomy. Her doctor just performed a hysterectomy, and his patient (who is my moms childhood bestie) almost died during a hysterectomy. He did it the laser way, and punctured her intestines. Her intestines were left punctured for 10 hours after surgery. It wasn’t till her daughter who is an R.N. told the obygn that something was off about her mom. Her blood pressure was way off. The doctor did an MRI and back to surgery she had to go. She wound up in the ICU and is due for another surgery. This isn’t looking for my moms friend.

title: Day in the Life of an ICU Nurse | Night Shift Routine, come to work with me
body: Thank you for this. I can't believe that you actually have one patient a night.  I work in what they call a step-down ICU unit with lots of patients with trachs, ventilators, and tube feedings. Most nights we can have as many as 5 or 6 patients.  And for a new nurse that is really overwhelming. Am starting to think l may be on the wrong unit as a new nurse. But thank you for this. It's helped me with an idea of how to organize myself at work.

We saw everything featured in this video, but we also saw the fitness (?) sort of place, complete with that little gym and even a huge swimming pool. title: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA *CAMPUS TOUR* | Classrooms, Sorority Row, Stadium, Starbucks, Library, Dorms
body:
Many things changed, since I was a student at UoA (1977-1984), the ΔΔΔ house was across the street from where it is now and it was round.
Barnwell field, that we used to play soccer is now a parking lot.
But the ΔΓ anchor, even with it's new brass color (it was dark colored in my days) reminded me of a time that our ΘΤ (back then it was a men's fraternity and ΔΓs were our Little Sisters, I think this changed now because in the new form ΘΤ became coed because is was a professional engineering fraternity and I believe the old scheme was dissolved for a while) pledges hand cuffed me on the anchor and as our members were chasing them around, in order to get the handcuff's key to free me, they lost the key in the grass lol.
I got so mad with the whole thing, that I put the double side of the cuff my hand was tied with, over a blade near the top of the ΔΓ anchor and using my arm as a lever, I broke the pin of the hand cuffs and set my self free, with only minor cuts on the sides of my wrist.
So the ΔΓ anchor has it's history with me too lol.
author: @FLORATOSOTHON

title: UNIVERSITY OF ALABAMA *CAMPUS TOUR* | Classrooms, Sorority Row, Stadium, Starbucks, Library, Dorms
body: The side of the KD house is designed to look like their former house that was in the same spot. If you look at the house from that side view and look at a picture of the old house you would swear it is the same house (until you go around the corner and see the rest of the new giant house)
